LAW OFFICES
SHAPIRO, POSELL & CLOSE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MITCHELL S. SHAPIRO
RICHARD E. POSELL
RICHARD H. CLOSE
BARRY S. GLASER
MARTIN L. DRUM
LLOYD SPANDORF
DOUGLAS L. CARDEN
STEVEN KARLTON KOP
FRED L. OLDS
STEPHEN I. WALPER
JONATHAN J. PANZER
ROBERT C. TRASK
DAVID L. STEINBERG
JAMES C. PARTON
ERIC D. BERKOWITZ
JULIE J. BISCEGLIA
LISA R. SKAIST
LORI S. PISCONA
WILLIAM J. SHAFFERMAN
DARRIN F. MEYER

ONE CENTURY PLAZA, SUITE 2600
2049 CENTURY PARK EAST
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067

TELEPHONE
(213) 277-1818

CABLE ADDRESS
"SHAPLAW"

TELECOPIER: (213) 201-4776

OF COUNSEL
ELIOT G. DISNER
RAYMOND WALLENSTEIN

June 6, 1990

BY TELECOPIER - ORIGINAL MAILED

Joseph Garcia, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: City of Palm Springs -
    Rent Review Commissioner

Dear Mr. Garcia:

    This will confirm my request today to obtain a copy,
when issued, of the advice letter that is being prepared
by you concerning the above matter.

    In addition, to permit us to respond to the request by
the City Attorney of Palm Springs, this is to confirm
that you will be faxing to me a copy of the written
request from the City of Palm Springs for the advice
letter.

    Upon receipt of that letter we request a reasonable
opportunity to respond.

    Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        RICHARD H. CLOSE

RHC:cg:c
cc: Siegfried Siefkes, Esq.

LAW OFFICES

SHAPIRO, POSELL & CLOSE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MITCHELL S. SHAPIRO
RICHARD E. POSELL
RICHARD H. CLOSE
BARRY S. GLASER
MARTIN M. EDELSTEIN SPANDORF
DOUGLAS L. CAROEN
STEVEN KARLTON KOP
LESLIE SACKS
STEPHEN I. HALPER
JONATHAN J. PANZER
ROBERT C. TRASK
DAVID L. STEINBERG
ROBERT DRABKIN
ERIC D. BERKOWITZ
JULIE J. BISCIGLIA
LISA K. SKAIST
LORI S. RIBEONA
STEPHEN S. SUGARMAN
DARR N F. MEYER

OF COUNSEL:
ELIOT G. DISNER
RAYMOND WALLENSTEIN

Jun 25 8 5[illegible] CENTURY PLAZA, SUITE 2600
2049 CENTURY PARK EAST
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067

TELEPHONE
(213) 277-1818

CABLE ADDRESS
"SHAPLAW"

TELECOPIER (213) 201-4776

June 22, 1990

FEDERAL EXPRESS

Joseph Garcia, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Conflict of Interest of a Rent Review
    Commission - City of Palm Springs

Dear Mr. Garcia:

    In furtherance of the letter we sent you May 3, 1990,
this letter is in response to the oral advice we under-
stand you issued to the Assistant City Attorney of Palm
Springs, Siegfried H. Siefkes.

                        STATEMENT OF FACTS

    Our client, owner of the El Dorado Mobile Home Park,
filed an Application in July of 1989 with the Palm
Springs Rent Review Commission for a hardship rent in-
crease.

    Hearings began on the Application last February. One of
the Commissioners, who has attended meetings and partici-
pated in the hearings since February, is married to a
real estate broker who acquired a listing for the sale
of a mobile home in the El Dorado Mobile Home Park.

    Arguably, the Commission's decision on a rent increase
could affect the amount of commission the Commissioner's
husband would earn. Our client's financial interest may
be in conflict with the Commissioner. However, our client
seeks to resolve this matter efficiently and has waived
any financial conflicts he has with the Commissioner.

LAW OFFICES

Shapiro, Posell & Close
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

Joseph Garcia, Esq.
June 22, 1990
Page Two


It is our understanding that you orally advised Mr.
Siefkes that your initial thoughts are:

     1.(a)  "The listing of a property, in and of itself,
            does not create a conflict of interest, even
            though the decision of the Commission could
            significantly affect the sale price of the
            property listed, and thus the commission which
            the Commissioner's husband would receive.

     (b)  If a prospective buyer is procured for the
          listed mobile home, a conflict of interest
          would then exist. A prospective buyer is deemed
          procured if the prospective buyer makes a de-
          posit towards the purchase of the mobile home.

     2.     If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel
            for the mobile home owner petitioner and counsel
            for the tenants as real parties in interest
            cannot waive that conflict of interest and
            that Commissioner may not continue to partici-
            pate in the hearing."


                           OUR POSITION

If a buyer is procured before the decision on the
hardship rent increase Application, then the price of
the mobile home is fixed. The Commissioner's vote will
not affect the commission her husband receives. There is
no conflict since there is already an agreed upon price.

Furthermore, the Commissioner is not disqualified under
California Code of Regulations, Title 9, Section 87103;
since her financial interest is not distinguishable from
the general public.

The FPPC has determined that the rental property industry
and tenants constitute the general public with respect
to decisions implementing rent control measures. In re
Overstreet (1981) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.

In our analogous case, the tenants and mobile home park
comprise the general public as they did in In re
Overstreet. The effect of the Rent Review Commission's
vote on the hardship rent increase will not affect the

LAW OFFICES
Shapiro, Posell & Close
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

Joseph Garcia, Esq.
June 22, 1990
Page Three


tenants, or the mobile home park in a manner distinguishable from the Commissioner; either both parties benefit or neither benefits.

If the Commissioner votes against the hardship rent increase, her husband could receive a larger commission. However, that commission pales in comparison to the savings four hundred mobile home park tenants receive because of rent control. The tenants gain any advantages the Commissioner might indirectly receive. Because the general public benefits with the Commissioner, there is no conflict between them. Thus, the "general public" exception applies to the Commissioner's financial interests.

The FPPC has also allowed Council members to participate in a vote on enacting a rent control ordinance even though they owned rental units in the targeted area. In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.

In that case, the Council members had a direct conflict of interests with the tenants in the rent control area; if the ordinance passed, the tenants benefited. If the Council voted against the ordinance, then the Council members who owned rental units profited by raising rents. However, the FPPC did not find the Council members' financial interest distinguishable from that of the general public's because each Council member owned less than three rental units.

Unlike the parties in In re Ferraro, the tenants and Commissioner in our case are not in adversarial positions. Both parties improve financially, or neither does. The Commissioner's financial interest in our case clearly falls within the "general public" exception of Regulation 87103.

The only adverse party to the Commissioner is our client, who has the largest financial interest in the matter; if the tenants and Commissioner benefit, then our client suffers because he cannot increase the rent.

LAW OFFICES
SHAPIRO, POSELL & CLOSE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

Joseph Garcia, Esq.
June 22, 1990
Page Four


Because the Commissioner has invested countless hours on
this matter since the proceedings began in February, the
Commissioner's continued participation would be in the
Rent Review Commission's best interest. The five member
Commission would suffer if the Commissioner could not
contribute her experience and expertise in this matter.

Regulation 81003 states that the Political Reform Act
should be "liberally construed to accomplish its purpose". The Act purports to eliminate various political
improprieties. This public policy consideration does not
apply to our case. The spirit of the Act is not tainted
because complete information has been disclosed to the
interested parties. Because our client is the only adverse party to the Commissioner's financial interest,
his waiver would efficiently resolve this matter.

Siegfried H. Siefkes, Assistant City Attorney for Palm
Springs, informed our client that the Rent Review Commission is conducting a "quasi-judicial proceeding". In
quasi-judicial hearings, parties are allowed to waive
conflicts of interest. California Code of Civil Procedure
Section 170.3 allows for parties to waive the disqualification of judges.

The same policy should apply to local commissioners. If
the interested parties have knowledge of the conflict
and waive it, then the judge or commissioner can perform
their duties.

Since the Commissioner does not have a financial interest
distinguishable from the general public, it would be
efficient and fair to allow the Commissioner to finish
participating in this matter.

Thank you for your consideration of this matter. If you
have any questions, or are in need of any further
information, please do not hesitate to call. We look
forward to hearing from you shortly.

Very truly yours,

SHAPIRO, POSELL & CLOSE

[signature]

RICHARD H. CLOSE

RHC:cg:g
cc: Siegfried Siefkes

City of Palm Springs

City Attorney
619-323-8211

May 23, 1990

Joseph Garcia
Senior Commission Council
428 "J" Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Garcia:

This is a request for confirmation of the advise which you have
given to me via telephone concerning the following fact
situation.

The City of Palm Springs has a Rent Review Commission, the
members of which are appointed by the City Council. The duties
of this Commission include hearing and making decisions on
petitions for hardship rent increases which are brought by owners
of residential rental properties.

The Commission is now conducting hearings on a petition for
hardship rent increase which was filed by the owner of a major
mobile home park located here in the city.

The husband of one of the Commissioners is a real estate broker.
The Commissioners husband has a listing for the sale of one of
the mobile homes located in the mobile home park which is the
subject of this hearing. If the mobile home were sold for the
listed price, the Commissioners husband would receive a
commission of $660. The decision of the Commission could have a
significant effect on the sale price of the mobile home for counsel
for the petitioning mobile home park owner and counsel for the
tenants as real parties in interest have both stipulated that
they would waive any conflict in interest so that this
Commissioner would continue to participate in this hearing.

I have asked the following questions:

1. Does this fact situation create a conflict of interest
for this Commissioner?

Post Office Box 1786, Palm Springs, California 92263-1786

2. If the answer to question number one is yes, may counsel for the mobile home park owner as petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest waive this conflict of interest so that the Commissioner may continue to participate in this matter?

You have advised me that:

1(a). The listing of a property, in and of itself, does not create a conflict of interest, even though the decision of the Commission could significantly affect the sale price of the property listed and thus the commission which the Commissioners husband would receive.

(b) If a prospective buyer is procured for the listed mobile home, a conflict of interest would then exist. A prospective buyer is deemed procured if the prospective buyer makes a deposit towards the purchase of the mobile home.

2. If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel for the mobile home owner petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest can not waive that conflict of interest and that Commissioner may not continue to participate in the hearing.

Your confirmation letter concerning these questions will most appreciated and helpful.

Thank you much for working with me on this.

Very truly yours,


WILLIAM J. ADAMS
City Attorney

BY: [signature]
SIEGFRIED H. SIEPKES
Assistant City Attorney

sections.question:
1. Does this fact situation create a conflict of interest for this Commissioner?

2. If the answer to question number one is yes, may counsel for the mobile home park owner as petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest waive this conflict of interest so that the Commissioner may continue to participate in this matter?
sections.conclusion:
1(a). The listing of a property, in and of itself, does not create a conflict of interest, even though the decision of the Commission could significantly affect the sale price of the property listed and thus the commission which the Commissioners husband would receive.

(b) If a prospective buyer is procured for the listed mobile home, a conflict of interest would then exist. A prospective buyer is deemed procured if the prospective buyer makes a deposit towards the purchase of the mobile home.

2. If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel for the mobile home owner petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest can not waive that conflict of interest and that Commissioner may not continue to participate in the hearing.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Rent Review Commissioner has a conflict of interest when her husband, a real estate broker, has a listing for a mobile home in the park whose owner is petitioning for a hardship rent increase, and whether such a conflict can be waived by the parties.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that a mere property listing does not create a conflict of interest, but a conflict would exist if a prospective buyer is procured (evidenced by a deposit). If a conflict exists, it cannot be waived by the parties and the Commissioner must recuse herself.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Mixed document file containing: (1) law firm letters dated June 6 and June 22, 1990 responding to FPPC advice; (2) City Attorney's May 23, 1990 request letter to FPPC with oral advice summarized. The conclusion extracted is from the City Attorney's letter recounting FPPC's oral advice. The document is primarily incoming requests/correspondence rather than an FPPC formal advice letter. Some OCR errors present in transcription.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does this fact situation create a conflict of interest for this Commissioner?

2. If the answer to question number one is yes, may counsel for the mobile home park owner as petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest waive this conflict of interest so that the Commissioner may continue to participate in this matter?

CONCLUSION: 1(a). The listing of a property, in and of itself, does not create a conflict of interest, even though the decision of the Commission could significantly affect the sale price of the property listed and thus the commission which the Commissioners husband would receive.

(b) If a prospective buyer is procured for the listed mobile home, a conflict of interest would then exist. A prospective buyer is deemed procured if the prospective buyer makes a deposit towards the purchase of the mobile home.

2. If a conflict of interest does exist, counsel for the mobile home owner petitioner and counsel for the tenants as real parties in interest can not waive that conflict of interest and that Commissioner may not continue to participate in the hearing.

embedding.summary: This file contains incoming requests and subsequent correspondence regarding a conflict of interest question for a Palm Springs Rent Review Commissioner whose husband has a real estate listing in a mobile home park whose owner is seeking a hardship rent increase. The City Attorney's initial request includes FPPC's oral advice on the matter, followed by a law firm response arguing against the conflict and waiver prohibition.
